content.problemStatement:
Read problems statements in Russian and Bengali.An array is called lovely if the sum of absolute differences of each adjacent pair of elements is odd; formally, the array SSS of size mmm is lovely if ∑i=1m−1\sum_{i=1}^{m-1}∑i=1m−1​ ∣Si−Si+1∣|S_i-S_{i+1}|∣Si​−Si+1​∣ is odd.

You are given an array AAA of NNN integers. You need to reorder the array in any manner such that the array becomes lovely. If there is no reordering operation that makes the array lovely, output `-1`.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The TTT test cases then follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing NNN integers denoting the modified array which is lovely, or `-1` if it is not possible.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line. If it is possible to reorder the array so that the sum of absolute differences of adjacent elements is odd, print any such ordering (N integers separated by a single space). If it is impossible, print -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case odds are {3,7,1} and evens are {10,2,12}. Placing all odds followed by all evens gives exactly one parity change, so the sum of absolute differences is odd. In the second test case both numbers are odd, therefore any ordering yields an even sum, so -1 is printed.
All numbers are even, so every adjacent difference is even; an odd total is impossible.
Odds:5,9,7; Evens:2,4. Concatenating odds then evens yields one parity change, making the total sum odd.

solution.approach: The parity of |a‑b| equals 1 iff a and b have different parity (one odd, one even). Therefore the total sum is odd exactly when the number of adjacent pairs with different parity is odd. If the array contains both odd and even numbers we can place all odds together followed by all evens (or the opposite). This creates exactly one parity change, hence an odd sum. If the array consists solely of odds or solely of evens, every adjacent difference is even and the sum can never be odd. So the solution is: • Separate the numbers into odd and even lists. • If both lists are non‑empty, output odds then evens. • Otherwise output -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, dominated by a single scan to separate numbers.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the two parity lists.
